nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving streams, side channels, and pools with leaf litter, submerged roots, and dense plant cover; associated with the Buxa (Buxar) Tiger Reserve area, Jalpaiguri District, West Bengal, northeastern India. Likely also ranges into the broader Brahmaputra drainage of northeastern India.

decorPreferences: driftwood and twigs for territory boundaries
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) for cover and tannins
caves or hollow wood for spawning sites

diet.notes: This species behaves as an obligate micro-predator in captivity, and dried foods are reliably refused — they should not be depended upon as a staple or even a supplement. The diet should consist of live or frozen small invertebrates: baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Daphnia, Cyclops, Moina, small bloodworm (Chironomus larvae), glass worms (Chaoborus larvae), and Mysis. Tubifex and large Chironomid larvae are best avoided. Breeders should be conditioned with a varied rotation of live foods in the weeks before spawning. Fry are capable of taking micro-worms and Artemia nauplii from the moment they become free-swimming.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids (microdevario, danio, celestial pearl danio)
small tetras with compatible water preferences
avoidWith: other male Badis of same or similar species (intense territorial aggression)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — predated as adults, neonates reliably eaten
boisterous or fast-moving feeders that outcompete at feeding time
aggressive cichlids or similarly territorial bottom-dwellers

summary: The Buxar Badis is an undescribed species from northeastern India, sold under the trade names ‘Buxar Badis’ and ‘Royal Rainbow Badis.’ Males display vivid, chameleon-like colour shifts during courtship and disputes. A reduced swim bladder gives this small fish a distinctive hopping gait along the bottom. It reliably refuses dried foods and requires live or frozen micro-prey.
careNotes:
Badis sp. ‘Buxar’ is an undescribed species circulating in the aquarium trade under the locality name ‘Buxar,’ which most evidence links to the Buxa Tiger Reserve area of Jalpaiguri District, West Bengal, northeastern India — not Buxar city in Bihar as sometimes reported. The fish is likely part of the broader B. badis species complex, which has been progressively split into described species including B. ruber, B. corycaeus, B. britzi, B. pyema, B. kyar, and others; a 2011 description of B. singenensis from the Singen River in Arunachal Pradesh may represent the same or a closely allied taxon, and the two populations have been treated as conspecific pending further study.

The most unusual feature of this species within the genus is its substantially reduced swim bladder, which renders it far more strictly bottom-bound than other Badis. It moves with a characteristic hopping action across the substrate and rarely ventures into open water, making it nearly impossible to confirm has eaten unless target-fed directly. A dimly lit, heavily structured tank of at least 54 L (60 × 30 × 30 cm) is the accepted minimum for a pair; larger tanks allow keeping one male with multiple females while reducing female harassment.

Water should be soft to moderately soft (gH 1–12) and slightly acid to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5). Natural seasonal temperature variation (down to ~16–18°C in winter) is beneficial and may trigger breeding condition in spring. Hard or strongly alkaline water is not tolerated long-term.

Feeding is the primary husbandry challenge: this species reliably refuses dried foods and must be maintained on live or frozen micro-invertebrates. Baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, Cyclops, and small bloodworm should form the staple. Individual fish occasionally learn to accept high-quality frozen bloodworm or Mysis but this cannot be assumed. Offer food directly to each fish if multiple are housed together, as males defend feeding spots. Tube-feeding with a pipette to each individual is a common keeper technique.

Males are highly territorial toward conspecific males; multiple males should not be housed in the standard minimum tank. The species is a cave spawner forming temporary pair bonds; males guard eggs and early fry with no participation from the female. Natural colour change (red flush, striping, contrasting ocellate markings) intensifies during courtship and territorial disputes.
breedingNotes: Cave spawner. Condition both sexes separately on varied live foods for 2–3 weeks before introducing. Provide multiple small spawning caves (coconut half-shells, clay pots, narrow PVC tubes) so the female has refuge after spawning. A gradual temperature rise of 2–3°C toward the upper range (24–26°C) combined with increased live-food feeding and small water changes mimicking seasonal conditions often triggers spawning. Female lays 30–100 eggs inside the chosen cave; the male then drives her away and guards the clutch alone. Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days; fry become free-swimming after a further 4–5 days. Remove female to a separate tank after spawning to prevent harassment. Feed fry on micro-worms (Panagrellus) and Artemia nauplii; paramecia useful for the first few days. Brood sizes are small enough that dedicated nano fry rearing is straightforward. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to live-food dependency and male aggression management.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Badis sp. ‘Buxar’
primary.notes: Primary source. Badis sp. ‘Buxar’ species profile with water parameters, tank requirements, diet, behaviour, breeding notes, and taxonomy discussion. SeriouslyFish also published a dedicated article on the B. singenensis / sp. Buxar relationship (https://www.seriouslyfish.com/badis-singenensis-the-most-unique-badid-gets-a-name/) confirming the Buxa Tiger Reserve, West Bengal collection site and 45 mm TL max size for males.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Badis singenensis (= sp. Buxar)
additional.notes: Treats B. singenensis and sp. Buxar as conspecific. Confirms 4–5 cm adult size, 14–30°C temperature tolerance, pH 6–8.5, soft-to-hard hardness, bottom-hopping behaviour due to reduced swim bladder, and typical cave-breeding cycle. Wholesale distributor with direct import experience.

additional.name: FishBase — Badis singenensis
additional.notes: Provides formal taxonomy (Badidae, Anabantiformes), type locality (Singen River, Arunachal Pradesh, India), and maximum recorded standard length of 3.7 cm SL for males. Used for taxonomy fields and as cross-reference for adult size range.

additional.name: Quality Marine — Buxar Badis
additional.notes: Trade distributor profile confirming undescribed status, Buxa National Park provenance, cave-spawning (up to 100 eggs), dry-food refusal, and male quarrelling behaviour. Identifies B. singenensis as the candidate formal name.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Badis sp. Buxar
additional.notes: UK fishkeeping magazine feature on this species in trade. Corroborates soft-acid water, live-food requirement, species-tank recommendation, and small adult size (~5 cm). Could not be fetched directly (HTTP 403) but URL confirmed via search indexing; data cross-checked against other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: A cave spawner in which the male guards 30–100 eggs alone after driving the female away from the spawning site. Conditioning on live foods and a gradual temperature increase toward the upper range typically triggers spawning. Fry accept micro-worms and Artemia nauplii from the free-swimming stage. Breeding is rated moderate difficulty, owing to the live-food dependency and the need to separate the female promptly after spawning to prevent ongoing harassment.
